back: A fast survival pathway that can trigger fight-or-flight reactions during social conflict.
Adrenaline, norepinephrine, and cortisol can increase arousal while reducing reflective thinking.
Calling someone difficult too quickly can reflect bias and prevent understanding of why behavior happened.
A four-part model: explain behavior, predict behavior, influence behavior, and control your own behavior.
Using words like we and us to frame conflict as a shared communication problem rather than a personal attack.
Specific positive acknowledgment can reinforce constructive behavior and improve difficult relationships.
Deep breathing and short pauses help calm the nervous system so you can respond more deliberately.
Separate identity from action: challenge harmful behavior without defining the whole person as bad.
